rejects.reason: Self-machinery false positive: .claude/settings.json is Reviewgate's OWN hook installation (the repo-local-hooks = code-execution pattern is the opted-into Reviewgate design, not a vuln in the reviewed change). It is a local uncommitted artifact, not introduced by this diff. Already fixed in source by commit a7b33ab (M-B5c, I-17), which excludes .claude/ via isExcludedFromReview + EXCLUDE_PATHSPEC; the running gate binary is a stale pre-rebuild dist, so the orchestrator drop-filter has not picked it up yet.

Self-machinery false positive (I-17): .claude/settings.json is Reviewgate's OWN hook installation. In THIS repo it is UNTRACKED-LOCAL (kept uncommitted), so the 'committing → RCE on clone' premise is false — `git log -- .claude/settings.json` shows nothing. Committed repo-local hooks are Reviewgate's INTENTIONAL opt-in (the gate runs for contributors by design), not a vuln to block on. The M-B5c diff-aware exclusion demotes OFF-diff .claude findings; an untracked-local file lands IN the working-tree diff, so it is now gitignored (.claude/settings.json + .local) to keep the gate's own machinery out of self-review.

By design (D-1 fail-safe-degrade, docs/dev/2026-06-03-multi-agent-feedback-remediation.md §3, user-approved): on lock CONTENTION the gate defers (allow_stop + deferred.flag) instead of blocking. The prior block-on-contention WAS the reported bug — it busy-looped and starved every parallel session. Eventual review is guaranteed: deferred.flag forces the next stop to take the lock (stopHasNothingToReview returns false while it exists) and the dirty.flag persists. Crucially only lock CONTENTION defers — every real review FAILURE (crash, timeout, error, quota, uncaught exception via runGateSafe) still FAILS CLOSED. Re-blocking would undo the approved decision and re-introduce the starvation.

O_NOFOLLOW already rejects a symlinked leaf (the realistic vector). A regular-file/hardlink swap needs pre-existing repo write access PLUS a protected_hardlinks bypass (kernel default-on, root-owned target); the read bytes are NEVER surfaced (only lineCount() is computed then discarded); and the worst case, skipping demotion, leaves the finding BLOCKING. Fail-safe, no content leak, no fail-open.

validateFindingFacts is a DEMOTE-ONLY safety net. A deleted file's line count is unverifiable because the file is gone from the working tree, so skipping it leaves the finding at its reviewer severity for the other layers (consensus/critic/grounding/reputation/singleton-failsafe) exactly as before P2 existed. That is fail-safe (the finding stays blocking), not blind trust, and never causes a fail-open.

validateFindingFacts is a DEMOTE-ONLY safety net. A deleted file's line count is unverifiable because the file is gone from the working tree, so skipping it leaves the finding at its reviewer severity for the other layers (consensus/critic/grounding/reputation/singleton-failsafe) exactly as before P2 existed. That is fail-safe (the finding stays blocking), not blind trust, and never causes a fail-open.

git rejects '..'-traversal components in tree/index entries and ls-files enumerates only in-repo paths, so collectDiff cannot emit '../../etc/...' in a diff header. And the deletedPaths branch performs ZERO filesystem access (it only short-circuits a finding to unchanged), so there is nothing to traverse; worst case the finding stays CRITICAL (fail-safe).

Same incoherent premise as F-005: git will not produce '..'-traversal paths in a diff header, and deletedPaths is used only to SKIP a finding (no filesystem access). The fact-check is demote-only, so a path it cannot validate leaves the finding blocking (fail-safe), never a bypass.

prune already skips symlinks: readdirSync withFileTypes Dirent.isDirectory() is false for a symlink (lstat semantics), so a planted YYYY symlink is ignored; auditDir is trusted config and rmSync only targets real date-dirs inside auditDir — no traversal outside it.

Intended defense-in-depth: redacting a hex value assigned to a secret-named key before it reaches an external reviewer is correct even for test fixtures; bare 40/64-char git SHAs are deliberately preserved (documented). The reviewer itself classifies this as a false-positive risk, not a security vulnerability.

Strip-by-default is the intended security posture with a keepKeys allowlist escape hatch; the cited examples API_VERSION and *_API_URL do NOT match /API[_-]?KEY/. CLI reviewers authenticate via OAuth credential files, not env vars, so dropping foreign secret-shaped env vars cannot break them.

No race: Node stream data handlers run serialized on the single-threaded event loop, so each runs to completion and sets outputTruncated before the next fires. The cap is honored exactly — only the remaining bytes are written before the flag and kill. No privilege boundary exists.

cwd and tmpdir always exist and realpath on the first loop iteration, so the FS-root fallback is unreachable for the containment roots; resolve() normalizes every .. so no parent-escape survives, and realpathSync of the nearest existing ancestor resolves all intermediate symlinks (a non-existent tail has no symlinks). Dev-only REVIEWGATE_CASSETTE feature.

redactStrings is pure: for objects it builds a new Record, for arrays it maps to a new array, for strings it returns a new string — it never mutates partial.result. The orchestrator receives the original unredacted object; only the on-disk cassette copy is redacted. The grounding judge already confirmed this.
